revision.text:
{{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|[[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[Buddhism in Japan]] * [[Dharmaguptaka]] * [[Schools of Buddhism]] * [[Vinaya]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31

{{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|[[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[Buddhism in Japan]] * [[Dharmaguptaka]] * [[Schools of Buddhism]] * [[Vinaya]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31

{{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|[[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[Buddhism in Japan]] * [[Dharmaguptaka]] * [[Schools of Buddhism]] * [[Vinaya]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31

{{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|[[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[Buddhism in Japan]] * [[Dharmaguptaka]] * [[Schools of Buddhism]] * [[Vinaya]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31

{{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|[[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[Nara, Nara|Nara]]. During the [[Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[Meiji period]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh_yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1351551]] Wikidata name: Risshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[:en:Vinaya|Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[:en:Dharmaguptaka|Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[:en:Kaidan-in|Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] * [[:en:Dharmaguptaka|Dharmaguptaka]] * [[:en:Schools of Buddhism|Schools of Buddhism]] * [[:en:Vinaya|Vinaya]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31 {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Japan-reli-stub}}

[[Category:Schools of Buddhism founded in Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Nara period]]
}}

Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[:en:Kaidan-in|Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] * [[:en:Dharmaguptaka|Dharmaguptaka]] * [[:en:Schools of Buddhism|Schools of Buddhism]] * [[:en:Vinaya|Vinaya]]

{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the [[:en:Vinaya|Vinaya]] textual framework of the [[:en:Dharmaguptaka|Dharmaguptaka]], one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[:en:Kaidan-in|Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] * [[:en:Dharmaguptaka|Dharmaguptaka]] * [[:en:Schools of Buddhism|Schools of Buddhism]] * [[:en:Vinaya|Vinaya]]

Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest [[:en:Jianzhen|Jianzhen]], better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]] in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]]. During the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, [[:en:Kaidan-in|Kaidan-in]], [[Saidai-ji]], and [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]]. However, during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only [[:en:Tōshōdai-ji|Tōshōdai-ji]], which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]] * [[:en:Dharmaguptaka|Dharmaguptaka]] * [[:en:Schools of Buddhism|Schools of Buddhism]] * [[:en:Vinaya|Vinaya]]

Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen}}, better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh-yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh-yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh-yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1351551]] Wikidata name: Risshū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|WD=Q848764|lt=Vinaya}} textual framework of the {{ill|Dharmaguptaka|en|Dharmaguptaka|WD=Q2445824|lt=Dharmaguptaka}}, one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen}}, better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, {{ill|Kaidan-in|en|Kaidan-in|WD=Q3191941|lt=Kaidan-in}}, [[Saidai-ji]], and {{ill|Sennyū-ji|en|Sennyū-ji|WD=Q1060966|lt=Sennyū-ji}}. However, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji}}, which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} * {{ill|Dharmaguptaka|en|Dharmaguptaka|WD=Q2445824|lt=Dharmaguptaka}} * {{ill|Schools of Buddhism|en|Schools of Buddhism|WD=Q222516|lt=Schools of Buddhism}} * {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|WD=Q848764|lt=Vinaya}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31 {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Japan-reli-stub}}

{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|WD=Q848764|lt=Vinaya}} textual framework of the {{ill|Dharmaguptaka|en|Dharmaguptaka|WD=Q2445824|lt=Dharmaguptaka}}, one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen}}, better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, {{ill|Kaidan-in|en|Kaidan-in|WD=Q3191941|lt=Kaidan-in}}, [[Saidai-ji]], and {{ill|Sennyū-ji|en|Sennyū-ji|WD=Q1060966|lt=Sennyū-ji}}. However, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji}}, which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan}} * {{ill|Dharmaguptaka|en|Dharmaguptaka|WD=Q2445824|lt=Dharmaguptaka}} * {{ill|Schools of Buddhism|en|Schools of Buddhism|WD=Q222516|lt=Schools of Buddhism}} * {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|WD=Q848764|lt=Vinaya}}
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Bibliography== * Bunyiu Nanjio (1886). [https://archive.org/details/shorthistoryoftw00nanjrich A short history of the twelve Japanese Buddhist sects], Tokyo: Bukkyo-sho-ei-yaku-shupan-sha, pp. 20–31 {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Japan-reli-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh-yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh-yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Risshū]][[en:Ritsu-shū]][[es:Ritsu]][[fr:Lüzong]][[ja:律宗]][[ko:율종]][[no:Risshū]][[pt:Ritsu]][[ru:Риссю]][[th:วินัย (นิกาย)]][[uk:Риссю]][[vi:Luật tông]][[zh-yue:律宗]][[zh:律宗]]
{{Short description|School of Nara Buddhism}} {{Refimprove|date=June 2016}} [[File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref14n4065.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}]]
{{Japanese Buddhism}} {{nihongo|'''Risshū'''|律宗}}, also ''Ritsu school'', is one of the [[Nanto Rikushū|six schools of Nara Buddhism]] in Japan, noted for its use of the {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|WD=Q848764|lt=Vinaya|1=Vinaya}} textual framework of the {{ill|Dharmaguptaka|en|Dharmaguptaka|WD=Q2445824|lt=Dharmaguptaka|1=Dharmaguptaka}}, one of the early schools of Buddhism.<ref>{{cite book|editor1-last=Buswell|editor1-first=Robert Jr|editor2-last=Lopez|editor2-first=Donald S. Jr.|editor1-link=Robert Buswell Jr.|editor2-link=Donald S. Lopez, Jr.|title=Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism.|date=2013|publisher=Princeton University Press|location=Princeton, NJ|pages=716–717|isbn=9780691157863}}</ref> The Ritsu school was founded in Japan by the blind Chinese priest {{ill|Jianzhen|en|Jianzhen|WD=Q714061|lt=Jianzhen|1=Jianzhen}}, better known by his Japanese name ''Ganjin''. Ganjin traveled to Japan at the request of Japanese priests, and established the {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}} in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, the Ritsu sect was divided into schools at Tōshōdai-ji, {{ill|Kaidan-in|en|Kaidan-in|WD=Q3191941|lt=Kaidan-in|1=Kaidan-in}}, [[Saidai-ji]], and {{ill|Sennyū-ji|en|Sennyū-ji|WD=Q1060966|lt=Sennyū-ji|1=Sennyū-ji}}. However, during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the Ritsu sect was incorporated within the [[Shingon]] sect by decree of the Japanese government. Today only {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|WD=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}}, which resisted the government measures, retains its identity as a Ritsu temple.
==See also== * {{ill|Buddhism in Japan|en|Buddhism in Japan|WD=Q736311|lt=Buddhism in Japan|1=Buddhism in Japan}} * {{ill|Dharmaguptaka|en|Dharmaguptaka|WD=Q2445824|lt=Dharmaguptaka|1=Dharmaguptaka}} * {{ill|Schools of Buddhism|en|Schools of Buddhism|WD=Q222516|lt=Schools of Buddhism|1=Schools of Buddhism}} * {{ill|Vinaya|en|Vinaya|WD=Q848764|lt=Vinaya|1=Vinaya}}
